skill.name: web3-testing
skill.description: Guides comprehensive smart contract testing with Hardhat and Foundry, including unit tests, fuzzing, gas checks, coverage, mainnet forks, and verification workflows.
skill.summary: Build and review Web3 smart contract test suites with Hardhat and Foundry.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found command, network, filesystem, environment, and blocker patterns in SKILL.md. Semantic review shows these are primarily documentation examples for Web3 testing, but the examples do handle private keys, API keys, RPC endpoints, mainnet forks, and CI commands, so users need clear warnings. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious behavior was found.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Credential Handling in Test Configuration Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill includes examples that read RPC URLs, a private key, CoinMarketCap API key, and Etherscan API key from environment variables. This is common for Web3 tooling, but it can expose funds or service credentials if users copy the pattern into unsafe environments or CI logs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples explicitly reference private key and API key environment variables. The context is legitimate testing documentation, so the risk is misuse rather than confirmed exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: External Network and Mainnet Fork Usage
medium_findings.description: The skill demonstrates mainnet forking through external RPC providers and Hardhat network provider requests. This is expected for smart contract testing, but it can send project metadata to RPC providers and consume paid API quotas.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network behavior is directly shown in mainnet fork and provider request examples. The examples are domain-appropriate and do not show credential exfiltration.

low_findings.title: External Command Examples Require User Review
low_findings.description: The skill contains shell and CI examples that run Hardhat commands, install dependencies, compile contracts, execute tests, and upload coverage. These are standard developer actions, but users should review generated commands before execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible documentation examples and are not executed by the skill itself. They still affect local projects or CI when copied by a user.

low_findings.title: Static Filesystem and Blocker Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported path traversal at line 168 is a normal Solidity relative import in a test example. The reported C2 and weak-cryptography blocker alerts appear to match ordinary Web3 testing terms and contract-test examples, with no evidence of malicious control channels or weak cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious locations are inside illustrative Solidity and JavaScript test snippets. I found no prompt injection text, command-and-control behavior, or cryptographic algorithm implementation at these locations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Private Key and API Key Environment Variables
dangerous_patterns.description: Example configuration references private key and API key environment variables. Users should use test-only keys, secret managers, and isolated CI permissions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The variables are explicitly named as credential-bearing values. The skill does not transmit them except through normal tool configuration examples.

dangerous_patterns.title: Mainnet Forking Through External RPC Providers
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples use mainnet fork providers and hardcoded mainnet contract addresses. Users should understand provider trust, rate limits, and privacy exposure before running these tests.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly perform or configure mainnet fork access. This is expected Web3 behavior, but it has operational and privacy implications.

content.value_statement: Smart contract teams need repeatable tests before code reaches mainnet. This skill guides Hardhat and Foundry test suites for unit, fork, fuzz, gas, coverage, and verification workflows.
actual_capabilities: Outline Hardhat configuration for Solidity tests, coverage, gas reporting, and Etherscan verification.
Show Foundry test patterns for fixtures, fuzzing, cheatcodes, and mainnet forks.
Guide unit test creation for transfers, events, access control, time logic, and revert conditions.
Suggest integration test structures for DeFi protocols that depend on existing mainnet contracts.
Provide CI examples for compiling contracts, running tests, measuring coverage, and publishing reports.
limitations: It does not audit production smart contracts for all vulnerabilities.
It assumes users already have Hardhat, Foundry, and Solidity project context.
Mainnet fork examples require external RPC providers and may use paid quotas.
Credential examples must be adapted with secure secret handling before real use.

use_cases.title: Create Unit Tests for a Token Contract
use_cases.description: Generate focused tests for deployment state, transfers, events, reverts, and time-based behavior.

use_cases.title: Validate a DeFi Integration on a Fork
use_cases.description: Plan tests that interact with real mainnet contracts while keeping execution local and repeatable.

use_cases.title: Standardize CI Testing for Contracts
use_cases.description: Build a workflow that compiles contracts, runs tests, measures coverage, and reports gas usage.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic Test Suite
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new Solidity project.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a basic Hardhat test plan for my Solidity contract. Cover deployment, owner permissions, transfers, events, and expected reverts.

prompt_templates.title: Add Foundry Fuzz Tests
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this contract behavior and propose Foundry fuzz tests. Include assumptions, boundary cases, and failure conditions to validate.

prompt_templates.title: Design Mainnet Fork Tests
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced integration testing against deployed protocols.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a mainnet fork test strategy for this DeFi integration. Identify contracts to impersonate, balances to seed, and external RPC risks.

prompt_templates.title: Harden CI for Contract Testing
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a CI testing strategy for this smart contract repository. Include compile, test, coverage, gas reporting, secret handling, and failure gates.

output_examples.input: Ask for tests for an ERC-20 style transfer function.
output: A test checklist for balances, events, insufficient balance reverts, zero-address handling, and authorization boundaries.
A fixture strategy that deploys the token once and reuses clean snapshots across cases.
Notes about testing decimals, total supply, and gas limits.

output_examples.input: Ask for a mainnet fork test plan for a swap integration.
output: A fork setup plan using a fixed block number for repeatable results.
A list of external contracts, whale accounts, token balances, and impersonation steps to verify.
Warnings about RPC provider privacy, quotas, and test-only credentials.

best_practices: Use test-only private keys and keep all real credentials out of prompts, logs, and committed files.
Pin mainnet fork tests to fixed block numbers so results are repeatable.
Combine unit, fuzz, fork, gas, and coverage checks before contract deployment.
anti_patterns: Do not paste production private keys or paid API secrets into generated examples.
Do not rely only on happy-path unit tests for financial contract behavior.
Do not run generated shell or CI commands before reviewing their project impact.

faq.question: Does this skill replace a smart contract audit?
faq.answer: No. It helps build stronger tests, but a manual security audit is still needed for production contracts.

faq.question: Can it work with both Hardhat and Foundry?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes guidance for Hardhat, Foundry, Forge tests, and combined testing workflows.

faq.question: Does mainnet forking require network access?
faq.answer: Yes. Fork tests usually need an external RPC provider and may expose request metadata to that provider.

faq.question: Should I use a real private key in examples?
faq.answer: No. Use test-only keys, isolated wallets, and secret management for any environment that runs tests.

faq.question: Can this help with gas optimization?
faq.answer: Yes. It can suggest gas comparison tests and reporting workflows for Solidity implementations.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
